source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I have read the letter which you in your wisdom have written me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You inveigh against me, and, though you once praised me and called me true partner and brother, you now write books to summon me to reply to the charges with which you terrify me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I see that in you are fulfilled the words of Solomon: “In the mouth of the foolish is the rod of contumely,” and “A fool receives not the words of prudence, unless you say what is passing in his heart;” and the words of Isaiah: “The fool will speak folly, and his heart will understand vain things, to practise iniquity and speak falsehood against the Lord.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For what need was there for you to send me whole volumes full of accusation and malediction, and to bring them before the public, when in the end of your letter you threaten me with death if I dare to reply to your slanders—I beg pardon—to your praises?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For your praises and your accusations amount to the same thing; from the same fountain proceed both sweet and bitter.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I beg you to set me the example of the modesty and shamefacedness which you recommend to me; you accuse another of lying: cease to be a liar yourself.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I wish to give no one an occasion of stumbling, and I will not become your accuser; for I have not to consider merely what you deserve but what is becoming in me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I tremble at our Savior’s words.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “Whosoever shall cause one of these little ones that believe in me to stumble, it were better for him that a great mill stone were hanged about his neck and he were drowned in the depths of the sea;” and “Woe unto the world because of occasions of stumbling: for it must needs be that occasions arise; but woe to the man through whom the occasion cometh.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It would have been possible for me too to pile up falsehoods against you and to say that I had heard or seen what no one had observed, so that among the ignorant my effrontery might be taken for veracity, and my violence for resolution.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But far be it from me to be an imitator of you, and to do myself what I denounce in you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He who is capable of doing filthy things may use filthy words.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “The evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is evil; for out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You may count it as good fortune that one whom you once called friend but now accuse has no mind to make vile imputations against you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I say this not from any dread of the sword of your accusation, but because I prefer to be accused than to be the accuser, to suffer an injury than to do one.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I know the precept of the Apostle: “Dearly beloved avenge not yourselves but rather give place unto wrath: for it is written Vengeance is mine, I will repay saith the Lord.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Therefore, if thine enemy hunger feed him, if he thirst give him drink; for in so doing thou shalt heap coals of fire upon his head.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For he that avenges himself cannot claim the vindication of the Lord.
